# Summary {.section-divider}

## Final Takeaways

- Imitation learning provides a practical route to robot skill acquisition.
- Diffusion policies offer expressive multimodal action generation.
- Real-time deployment requires attention to inference latency.
- Contact-rich robotics also needs compliant control, not just motion prediction.
- Variable admittance is a natural bridge between learned behavior and physical interaction.

::: {.notes}
The lecture can be summarized as a chain from demonstrations, to generative policies, to compliant physical execution.

The policy proposes temporally coherent behavior, while compliance determines how that behavior survives contact uncertainty.

No single model solves everything. Deployment is a system-level problem.
:::

---

## Open Research Questions

- How many denoising steps are enough for stable robot behavior?
- Can learned compliance parameters be made certifiably safe?
- How should policies combine vision, force, proprioception, and EMG?
- What benchmarks best measure contact-rich manipulation quality?

::: {.notes}
These open questions push us beyond benchmark success rates.

A policy that succeeds 90 percent of the time may still be unacceptable if the failures involve unsafe contact.

"Safe learning" claims need careful definitions of safety, uncertainty, and deployment conditions.
:::

---

## Discussion Questions

- When would a deterministic policy be preferable to a diffusion policy?
- What failure modes are unique to learned compliance?
- Should a policy output low-level control parameters directly?
- How should we trade off task success, safety, and interpretability?

::: {.notes}
These questions are about design trade-offs rather than recall.

One way to approach them is to argue for conservative architectures first, and only then identify what performance they might lose.

The discussion should stay grounded in robot constraints, not only in machine-learning model capacity.
:::

---

## References

::: {.references-grid}
::: {}
- Abu-Dakka, F. J., & Saveriano, M. (2020). Variable Impedance Control and Learning — A Review. *Frontiers in Robotics and AI*, 7, 590681.
- Bacciu, D. *Imitation Learning*. Lecture material, Università di Pisa.
- Bishop, C. M. (1994). *Mixture Density Networks*. Tech. Report NCRG/94/004, Aston University.
- Buchli, J., Stulp, F., Theodorou, E., & Schaal, S. (2011). Learning Variable Impedance Control. *Int. J. Robotics Research*, 30(7), 820–833.
- Chi, C., Xu, Z., Feng, S., et al. (2025). Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion. *Int. J. Robotics Research*, 44(10–11), 1684–1704.
- Ho, J., Jain, A., & Abbeel, P. (2020). Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models. *NeurIPS*, 33, 6840–6851.
- Hogan, N. (1985). Impedance Control: An Approach to Manipulation, Parts I–III. *J. Dynamic Systems, Measurement, and Control*, 107(1), 1–24.
:::

::: {}
- Ott, C., Mukherjee, R., & Nakamura, Y. (2010). Unified Impedance and Admittance Control. *IEEE ICRA*, 554–561.
- Pomerleau, D. A. (1988). ALVINN: An Autonomous Land Vehicle in a Neural Network. *NeurIPS*, 1, 305–313.
- Pomponi, V. (2026). *Generative AI with Diffusion: An Introduction to Diffusion Models*. Lecture material, SUPSI / Politecnico di Milano.
- Ross, S., & Bagnell, D. (2010). Efficient Reductions for Imitation Learning. *AISTATS*, 661–668.
- Ross, S., Gordon, G. J., & Bagnell, J. A. (2011). A Reduction of Imitation Learning and Structured Prediction to No-Regret Online Learning. *AISTATS*, 627–635.
- Song, J., Meng, C., & Ermon, S. (2021). Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models. *ICLR*.
- Villani, L., & De Schutter, J. (2016). Force Control. In *Springer Handbook of Robotics* (2nd ed., pp. 195–220). Springer.
- Whitney, D. E. (1987). Historical Perspective and State of the Art in Robot Force Control. *Int. J. Robotics Research*, 6(1), 3–14.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
The references are organized around the main blocks of the lecture. Ross and Bagnell cover imitation learning and covariate shift; Ho and Song cover diffusion sampling; Chi et al. cover Diffusion Policy; and Hogan, Ott, Villani, Whitney, Abu-Dakka, Saveriano, Buchli, Stulp, Theodorou, and Schaal cover force, impedance, admittance, and variable-compliance control.

For deeper study, the useful move is to read across categories instead of staying within one field. The lecture only makes sense when the generative-model view and the robot-control view are combined. The project-specific case study should also be treated as preliminary where unpublished numbers are shown; the mature literature is represented by these references.
:::

---

## Thank You for Your Attention

::: {.equation-card}
**Alessandro Leanza**

alessandro.leanza@supsi.ch

github.com/alessandro-leanza
:::

::: {.notes}
My contact details are here for questions, follow-up, and repository access. The final message is practical: the lecture has connected demonstrations, diffusion-based action generation, real-time inference, and compliant control, but the strongest message is that robot learning is a system problem. A good learned policy still needs the right controller interface, timing budget, sensing stack, and safety envelope.

Good closing questions connect the two halves of the lecture: where the generative policy should stop, where the controller should take over, and what evidence would convince us that learned compliance is actually safer or more effective than a conservative hand-designed controller.
:::
